parameters.content:
### Hourly Schedule

Starts the workflow on a timer — every 6 h. No input data; downstream nodes fetch what they need.

Next: Map to Canonical Schema

### Map to Canonical Schema

Shapes the item: writes `jobId`, `kind`, `payload`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Route by Event Kind

### Route by Event Kind

Routes each item to one of 3 lanes: job.start, job.done + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Handle 'job.start' → Notion · ②→ Handle 'job.done' → Forward API · ③→ Log Unrecognized Kind

### Handle 'job.start' → Notion

Creates a new page in the Notion database — the title is pulled straight from the item's key field.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Notion integration token

### Handle 'job.done' → Forward API

POST https://api.example.com/v1/jobs — retries 3× with backoff on failure.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 API key / bearer for the target API

### Log Unrecognized Kind

Creates a new page in the Notion database — the title is pulled straight from the item's key field.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Notion integration token

### Consolidate Handling

Rejoins 3 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Stamp Processing Time

### Stamp Processing Time

Stamps `processedAt` with the current time for auditing.

Next: Guard Non-Empty type

### Guard Non-Empty type

Drops items that fail the condition; only qualifying items continue (no false branch).

Next: Sort Jobs Newest First

### Sort Jobs Newest First

Orders items (newest first) so limits and digests pick the most relevant records.

Next: Save Execution Metadata

### Save Execution Metadata

Saves key fields onto the execution record — searchable later in n8n's execution list.

Next: Cap Batch Volume

### Cap Batch Volume

Caps the stream at 100 items to protect downstream quotas.

Next: Summarize Run Stats

### Summarize Run Stats

Shapes the item: writes `runAt`, `itemCount`, `status`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Append Audit Row (Notion)

### Append Audit Row (Notion)

Creates a new page in the Notion database — the title is pulled straight from the item's key field.

Ports: ①→ Send Summary to Gmail · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Notion integration token

### Send Summary to Gmail

Sends an email via Gmail — subject is the first line of the message body, recipient comes from the item's `email` field.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Slack

### Escalate Failure to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#automation`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

# Automate a routine task with HTTP / REST API and Notion and log the results

**Pattern:** Workflow Automation · **Level:** Advanced · **Trigger:** Scheduled

## What it does
Automate a routine task with HTTP / REST API and Notion and log the results. It runs automatically on a schedule.

## Why it exists
Manual triggers is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Hourly Schedule → Map to Canonical Schema → Route by Event Kind → Handle 'job.start' → Notion → Consolidate Handling → Stamp Processing Time → Guard Non-Empty type → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to workflow automation without manual effort
- Anyone automating triggers in productivity
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Keeps your systems in sync
- Scales without extra headcount
- Improves response time

## Before first run
Connect credentials: API key / bearer for the target API; Google OAuth2; Notion integration token; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

The optional HMAC check runs before any business logic touches the payload — bad signatures are rejected at the door rather than after work has already been done with untrusted data. Acking fast (HTTP 202) and doing the real work after the response is what stops the sender's webhook delivery from timing out and re-delivering the same event.

## Practice exercises

You've got the basics covered — push further:

1. Turn on signature verification and trace what a tampered payload does at the 'Signature Valid?' gate.
2. Add a new event kind to the switch and give it its own handler.
3. Remove the fast-ack step and observe how response time changes under a slow downstream write.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your triggers process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
